purpose: Show how a hypothetical company could use OpenCompliance to publish corridor-scoped proof, attestation, and failure artifacts without pretending that whole standards are fully proved.

workflow.detail: Split the company posture into scoped corridors such as cyber hygiene, issued mixed controls, AI governance, and blocked or stale slices.
Publish machine-state exports where possible and keep documentary governance evidence as signed attestation claims.
Generate corridor-specific proof bundles, trust-surface reports, verification results, and either a scoped certificate or a typed punch-list.
Expose trust-surface reports, replay bundles, witness receipts, transparency logs, and OSCAL-shaped projections without leaking private operational data.

workflow.step: Let third parties inspect and replay
workflow.detail: Give buyers and reviewers a package they can inspect corridor by corridor instead of one flattened compliance claim.

frameworkPositions.Cyber Essentials: Narrow public cyber hygiene corridor with clean machine-checkable examples, not a full certification claim.
frameworkPositions.EU AI Act: Documentary AI-governance corridor plus one machine-checkable disclosure control, not full legal compliance.
frameworkPositions.EU GPAI Code of Practice: Transparency-focused AI example corridor only, not complete code adherence.
frameworkPositions.GDPR: Framework-adjacent family mappings appear in the public cyber corridor, but adequacy and legal interpretation remain outside the proved slice.
frameworkPositions.IRAP: Family-level overlap only in the current public example packs, not a complete IRAP assessment.
frameworkPositions.ISO 27001: Family-proxy and corridor-scoped examples only, not clause-level certification or full Annex A coverage.
frameworkPositions.ISO/IEC 23894: AI risk guidance appears in the AI-governance example as attested process evidence, not a complete standard implementation.
frameworkPositions.ISO/IEC 42001: AI management system family mappings appear in the AI-governance corridor, but the public slice stays narrow and mixed.
frameworkPositions.ISO/IEC 42005: Impact-assessment-related family mappings appear in the AI-governance corridor, not a complete impact assessment regime.
frameworkPositions.NCSC CAF 4.0: Crosswalked family-level support only in the cyber baseline corridor.
frameworkPositions.NIST AI RMF 1.0: AI governance and transparency families appear in the synthetic AI corridor, still mostly attestation-backed.
frameworkPositions.NIST CSF 2.0: Crosswalked family-level support only in the cyber baseline corridor.
frameworkPositions.NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5.1: Crosswalked family-level support only in the cyber baseline corridor.
frameworkPositions.SOC 2: Narrow corridor evidence only, not a full opinion or clause-complete public mapping corpus.

corridors.title: Cyber baseline corridor
corridors.objective: Show the cleanest machine-checkable path for a narrow security hygiene baseline.
corridors.safeStatement: ExampleCo can state that the managed admin and endpoint baseline enforces MFA, default-deny network boundaries, secure configuration, timely patching, and malware protection for the scoped fleet.
corridors.whyIncluded: This is the simplest corridor for buyers who want to see what a genuinely provable slice looks like today.
corridors.intendedAudience: Security buyers and baseline assurance reviewers

corridors.title: Issued mixed corridor
corridors.objective: Show a certificate-eligible corridor that combines machine evidence with signed operational attestations.
corridors.safeStatement: ExampleCo can state that the scoped identity, logging, access-review, training, restore, and incident-runbook corridor has no blocking gaps, while still disclosing which parts are proved and which parts are attested.
corridors.whyIncluded: This demonstrates the most realistic near-term success path: some controls are proved, others remain documentary, and the certificate still stays narrow.
corridors.intendedAudience: Audit-readiness partners, trust reviewers, and serious buyers

corridors.title: AI governance corridor
corridors.objective: Show what an honest AI governance package looks like when most of the corridor is still documentary.
corridors.safeStatement: ExampleCo can state that AI context, risk process, oversight, and monitoring are documented and signed, and that AI-generated content disclosure is mechanically demonstrated on the scoped assistant surface.
corridors.whyIncluded: This keeps AI marketing honest by separating documentary governance evidence from the one narrow technical control that is currently machine-checkable.
corridors.intendedAudience: AI governance reviewers, privacy reviewers, and enterprise buyers

corridors.title: Blocked corridor
corridors.objective: Show how OpenCompliance behaves when a corridor should not issue.
corridors.safeStatement: ExampleCo can state that this corridor is not issuance-ready, and can hand over a typed punch-list showing exactly which controls failed or still require human judgment.
corridors.whyIncluded: A trustworthy framework must show honest failure paths rather than only polished success cases.
corridors.intendedAudience: Auditors, internal control owners, and buyers who care about remediation clarity
